column29: There's little to no incentive to try to point out that a high-rated answer is out of date or wrong. Similarly, there's little to no motivation to provide a more in-depth answer that covers more of the original question if there's a short answer posted years ago. Answers passively accrue more upvotes over time, so an answer that is technically correct but uninteresting might have hundreds of points. So, it seems like SO is trying to double-dip in "answer someone's question quickly enough that they can use the answer" and also "provide a community resource" without doing either one very well.

column29: The big problem is that it's hard to get started. In order to accrue a decent amount of rep, you either need to find a question that's just been asked and not got any good answers yet, that's in a visible-enough category/tags to garner a decent number of upvotes; or you need to *ask* a question that's frequently-asked enough to gather a good amount of rep, but not frequently-asked enough to have already been asked so your question doesn't get marked as a duplicate. Both of these are basically down to luck! (Or for the first one, obsessive refreshing of the tags where you have expertise.) And if you don't do either of those, you sit and very very slowly gather rep for niche answers and niche questions (which most questions are) which garner you one or two upvotes. The system probably used to work, years ago, but it doesn't now. Unless you get in near the start of some new trendy programming language asking the basic obvious questions which everyone's going to want to know...

column29: StackOverflow is full of assholes, but it's not the peoples fault, it's the system. While keeping SO a great place to get answers, DO NOT incentivize users to be rude towards people who are struggling!    People are freely shitting on struggling users because there is no cost of doing so, quite the contrary.  Solutions?  Make negative actions cost rep.  Downvoting will cost  Closing will cost.    You'd argue there's a fine line between a struggling user and blatant spam, but really there isn't. Don't reward users for treating struggling users like spammers.    Make StackOverflow friendly and helpful community.

Perhaps with a members only version of the site with only professionals, to filter out the copy/paste developers.

column29: There is little incentive for people asking questions to ask a good/complete question. The largest problem I have with SO is how poor the actual questions are. The titles, in many cases, aren't even questions. People don't do their research first. There should be a carrot/stick situation with questions beyond just up/downvote. Maybe new users don't get just an empty box to ask their question - maybe they need to fill out a larger form for their first two or three questions with information about what they've searched for, which documents they've read, etc.    The "long game" rarely trumps the "fastest gun in the west" problem. The accepted answer is usually the first one that barely answers the question, not actually the best answer.    There's no incentive for question askers to come back and mark an accepted answer. This gets forgotten a lot, especially by new users.
column30: I think the point system/gamification of the system is good, but it definitely skews incentives toward the people answering questions and misses the importance of asking a good question.

column29: I spent a lot of time on stack overflow years ago when there wasnt so many answered questions - but nowadays I dont actively try to answer questions there.  The culture of closing and belittling legitimate questions, mods on power trips and then the flip side of people asking low-effort or homework questions means that I am mostly a consumer of SO nowadays.      But, for me, it was never about the points themselves, it was about helping people and then the points allowed other people to trust that I had helped other people in the past so maybe I knew what I was talking about.    If you want more people answering questions, deal first with the culture and community - and then find different and better ways to expose questions that need to be answered.  For me anyway, badges and rankings dont incentivize, in fact I would say direct competition isnt usually helpful in terms of answering questions.    One of the best ways to provide value to people is to make a question a wiki, removing any competition - but that also means that people dont get any reward for their work.

column29: It seems unclear how to rank people by badges.  There's a multi-tier reputation system (badges, rep, stars, etc) but only one is ranked - reputation.  To people who think the reputation system is biased towards certain things (e.g. poor answers are worth more than great questions), this can seem like an unfortunate oversight.    So having rankings of things other than reputation might make some people feel validated for their particular contributions, particularly where they feel that the biases of the reputation system do not adequately reflect the value of their efforts.    So ... more rankings? ??

Nowadays, I visit from time to time and read a few things, but I rarely answer myself.

column29: The incentive mechanisms are irrelevant to me. Stack Overflow has been a very useful resource to me. I contribute to it not only to give back but also to help ensure that it remains useful.
column30: They're kind of fun in the beginning but eventually fade into background noise.

column29: I think the worst thing is that easy questions get the most answers even if it would be quite easy to get the same results by searching online, while the most complex ones usually do not get any answer at all.  It should be the very opposite.  So I highly encourage to build something that incentivizes answering complex questions (like 10x points, or some otherwise unavailable privilege). This is different from bounties. I don't like the idea of "paying" a bounty for a question. It somehow creates a wrong idea of helping.

column29: I guess it would be nice if you could up/downvote comments as well an answers -- some/sometimes comments are more helpful than the answers.
column30: This is just kind of humorous: Every so often I think, I'll go look at the newest Python questions and see if there are some I can answer, and sometimes I see one that is like one minute old, and I start typing an answer, and by the time I finish, either someone else has answered it already, or the question has been closed for being a duplicate or some other violation. No points for me -- things can move amazingly fast on Stack Overflow! :-)
